title: Linear equations in smooth numbers
speaker: Lilian Matthiesen
abstract: A number is called y-smooth if all of its prime factors are bounded above by y. The set of y-smooth numbers below x forms a sparse subset of the integers below x as soon as x is sufficiently large in terms of y. If f_1, …, f_r \in Z[x_1,…,x_s] is a system of pairwise Q-linearly independent linear forms, one may ask how often these forms simultaneously take values in the set of y-smooth numbers when the variables x_i are all restricted to be of size about x. I will discuss asymptotic results on this counting problem. This talk is based on joint work with Mengdi Wang.

proof_stages.label: Green--Tao transference framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls that asymptotics for linear correlations of arithmetic functions can be obtained by transferring to bounded functions using pseudorandom majorants and by proving high-order Gowers-uniformity, equivalently nilsequence orthogonality.

proof_stages.label: Smooth-number sparsity target and logarithmic barrier
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates the problem of finding smooth values of systems of linearly independent linear polynomials, explains why sparse smooth numbers are not covered by existing sieve or multiplicative-function methods, and argues that the smallest plausible range is y at least a sufficiently large power of log N.

proof_stages.label: Orthogonal smooth-supported weight as the first main theorem
proof_stages.summary: The required object is set up as a normalized weight g supported on y-smooth numbers whose centered version is orthogonal to all relevant nilsequences, and the announced theorem proves such a weight in the near-optimal range log^A N ≤ y ≤ N^ε, leaving the majorant problem separate.

proof_stages.label: Rough smooth numbers and short-progression equidistribution
proof_stages.summary: Because ordinary smooth numbers have a coprimality bias in residue classes, the proof removes small prime factors and then weights by the saddle-point factor n^{1-α} to obtain a candidate g that is equidistributed in arithmetic progressions and short intervals.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to equidistributed nilsequences and type-II sums
proof_stages.summary: A standard factorization/reduction reduces centered nilsequence orthogonality to cancellation against equidistributed nilsequences, and a strong logarithmic decomposition converts the resulting one-variable sum into bilinear type-II sums over primes and smooth variables.

proof_stages.label: Equidistribution after sparse smooth dilations
proof_stages.summary: After swapping summations, the proof must show that most dilated nilsequences remain equidistributed along the sparse rough-smooth set, which is achieved through Green--Tao quantitative Leibman theory, reduction to polynomial recurrence on tori, Weyl-sum bounds over smooth variables, and bootstrapping with arithmetic-progression estimates.

proof_stages.label: Consequences when a majorant is available
proof_stages.summary: Combining the nilsequence-orthogonal weight with available rough-number or sieve majorants in the dense or mildly sparse range yields expected-order counts for linear systems taking values in the rough smooth subset, while the fully sparse majorant remains work in progress.

proof_stages.label: Partial results without a full majorant
proof_stages.summary: The tempting induction using y+1-smooth weights as majorants fails because inverse theorems lose Gowers-norm order and quantitative strength, but a logarithmic decomposition of the smooth indicator still gives U^k bounds in a larger range and counts for hypercube-like configurations, motivating an open problem about small Gowers norms without correct progression counts.

examples.name: Schur equation a+b=c in smooth numbers
examples.note: The three-form count for n1, n2, n1+n2 is used to show both the expected-order heuristic and, via known results and the ABC obstruction for smaller y, why a logarithmic-power smoothness threshold is the best plausible target.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker rewrites a+b=c as the product over n1, n2, and n1+n2 and computes the expected number of smooth solutions using psi(n,y) factors and a probability factor

examples.name: The R/Z nilsequence as an exponential phase
examples.note: Taking the nilmanifold to be R/Z and the Lipschitz function to be e^{2πix} turns nilsequence orthogonality into the familiar problem of bounding smooth-number exponential sums, making the abstract theorem concrete without supplying the proof mechanism.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker identifies the R/Z nilmanifold case with exponential phases and cites related Harper bounds, but does not compute or verify anything on this example

passes.reason: The R/Z nilsequence is the one-dimensional abelian/1-step special case of a general nilsequence, used only illustratively.

examples.name: Hypercube-like linear configurations
examples.note: These Gowers-norm-shaped systems illustrate the limited class of configurations that can still be counted from the logarithmic decomposition and U^k bounds when no full pseudorandom majorant is available.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the hypercube-like configurations explicitly from two vectors and binary strings, then relates the resulting forms to Gowers-norm linear forms

examples.name: Four-term arithmetic progressions as a test problem
examples.note: The proposed search for a function with small high-order Gowers norms but the wrong four-term progression count illustrates what the missing linear-forms condition is meant to prevent.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker poses four-term arithmetic progressions as a possible test problem for small uniformity norms versus expected counts, without carrying out a concrete count or construction
